findings.title: Bulk DELETE SQL Injection via $_REQUEST['id'] - Claimed by sqli-002
findings.sanitization: absint() applied to every element of $_REQUEST['id'] in the loop at lines 386-388 before implode(). Each array value is cast to a non-negative integer, making SQL injection impossible.
findings.false_positive_reason: The taint scanner traced $_REQUEST['id'] directly to the SQL sink but missed the foreach loop at lines 386-388 that runs each element through absint(). After absint(), all values are guaranteed non-negative integers, making the imploded string safe for use in an IN() clause. Additionally, requires authenticated admin (manage_options) and valid nonce.

findings.title: Search SQLi via $_REQUEST['s'] - Claimed by sqli-001 and sqli-003
findings.sanitization: The $where variable is constructed exclusively by $wpdb->prepare() calls (lines 279-288 and 312-322). When $this->s is not a valid IP, prepare() uses %s placeholder; when it is a valid IP, ip2long() converts it to an unsigned integer used with %d. The taint scanner flagged $where as tainted, but $where was produced by wpdb->prepare() which fully parameterizes the query.
findings.nonce_check: This is a GET search on the admin list table; the page itself requires admin authentication
findings.false_positive_reason: The taint analysis incorrectly propagated the taint label through $wpdb->prepare(). $wpdb->prepare() is a sink-neutralizer for SQL injection - the output is safe SQL. The final query concatenates $where (already a safe prepared string) with other fixed strings, ORDER BY values validated against a whitelist (lines 140-155), numeric paged/per_page values, and a fixed LIMIT.

findings.title: XSS via echo on $_POST['gglcptch_add_to_allowlist'] isset check - Claimed by xss-001
findings.sanitization: The echo outputs only one of two hardcoded string literals based on a boolean isset() check. No user-supplied data is ever output. isset() returns true/false; the ternary then selects between ' style="display: none;"' or ''. There is no user input in the echoed values.
findings.false_positive_reason: The taint scanner incorrectly treated the isset() check on a POST variable as a taint source that propagates to the echo. The echo only outputs a fixed CSS attribute string or empty string - the user input controls which of two safe constants is output, not the content of the output itself.

findings.title: XSS via $_GET['page'] in URL construction - Claimed by xss-002 through xss-012
findings.sanitization: All ten sink points wrap their output in esc_url(). esc_url() is the correct and sufficient escaping function for URL attribute contexts (href). It strips disallowed URL protocols and HTML-encodes special characters. This fully prevents XSS in href attributes.
findings.false_positive_reason: The taint scanner reported these because esc_html() or esc_attr() was 'missing', but esc_url() is the correct escaping function for URL contexts. esc_url() sanitizes the URL and prevents XSS injection in href attributes by encoding special chars and blocking non-URL schemes like javascript:. These are standard WordPress best-practice URL outputs and do not constitute XSS vulnerabilities.

findings.title: SSRF via wp_remote_post body containing $_POST['g-recaptcha-response'] - Claimed by ssrf-001
findings.sanitization: The URL in wp_remote_post is a hardcoded constant 'https://www.google.com/recaptcha/api/siteverify'. User input from $_POST['g-recaptcha-response'] (sanitized by sanitize_text_field) appears only in the POST body as the 'response' field - it does not influence the target URL in any way. True SSRF requires user control over the URL or redirect target.
findings.capability_check: None required (this is the reCAPTCHA verification step, runs on form submission)
findings.false_positive_reason: The taint scanner flagged this because user-controlled data appears in $args which is passed to wp_remote_post. However, the POST body content does not constitute SSRF - only URL control creates SSRF. The hardcoded URL cannot be redirected or modified by user input.

findings.title: SSRF via bws_license_key in wp_remote_post body - Claimed by ssrf-002
findings.sanitization: The URL is hardcoded. The license key appears only in the POST body as serialized data. User input does not control the target URL. sanitize_text_field() is applied at the source.
findings.false_positive_reason: Same as GC-005: user data in the POST body does not constitute SSRF. The remote URL is a hardcoded string pointing to bestwebsoft.com. No user-controlled URL resolution occurs.

findings.title: LFI via DB option value in require_once - Claimed by lfi-001 and lfi-002
findings.sanitization: file_exists() check before require_once. Path originates from a database option written by the BWS Custom Code plugin, not from direct user HTTP input.
findings.capability_check: Function begins with if (is_admin()) return - only executes on frontend. Writing to bstwbsftwppdtplgns_options requires admin/DB access.
findings.false_positive_reason: The taint scanner treats get_option() as a taint source (correct per SSRF/SQLi doctrine), but the exploit chain requires: (1) an attacker to first write an arbitrary path to the 'bstwbsftwppdtplgns_options' DB option, and (2) that path to point to an existing attacker-controlled PHP file on the server filesystem. Step (1) requires admin/DB write access; anyone with that access already has arbitrary code execution via WP. Additionally, the file_exists() check is a real guard - the path must already exist on disk. This is not independently exploitable as LFI from user HTTP input.
